title: Faithful by Consensus: Identifying Causally Important Features Through Multi-Seed Sparse Autoencoder Agreement
description: We propose using multi-seed agreement as a signal for identifying causally faithful sparse autoencoder features. By training multiple SAEs with different random seeds on the same model and matching features across runs, we show that 'consensus features' (found in most runs) are significantly more causally important, more interpretable, and more useful for downstream tasks than 'singleton features' (found in only one run). We further construct 'consensus dictionaries' that provide better per-feature utility than any single SAE run.
motivation: Sparse autoencoders are the dominant tool for decomposing language model representations into interpretable features, but recent work shows that only ~30% of features are shared across training runs with different random seeds. This instability undermines claims that SAEs discover the model's 'true' features. While prior work established that stable features are more interpretable, the critical question of whether stability predicts causal importance — whether the model actually uses these features for its predictions — remains unanswered. Answering this question would provide both theoretical insight into what SAEs actually learn and a practical method for curating higher-quality feature dictionaries.
proposed_approach: Train N=10 SAEs with different random seeds at each of 3 layers of Pythia-160M. Match features across runs using the Hungarian algorithm on decoder cosine similarity. Assign each feature a 'consensus score' (fraction of runs recovering it). Evaluate features at different consensus levels on: (1) causal importance via feature ablation and KL divergence measurement, (2) sparse probing accuracy for linguistic properties, (3) feature steering effectiveness, and (4) auto-interpretability scores. Construct 'consensus dictionaries' by selecting representative vectors for high-consensus features and evaluate them on SAEBench metrics. Analyze whether singleton features correspond to manifold tilings by examining their spatial clustering in decoder weight space.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Trained on the Same Data Learn Different Features
related_work.relation: Directly establishes the feature instability problem that our work builds on. Shows only 30% feature overlap across seeds and that shared features are more interpretable. We extend this by connecting stability to causal importance and proposing consensus as a practical feature quality metric.

related_work.title: Scaling and Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: Leo Gao, Tom Dupre la Tour, Henk Tillman, Gabriel Goh, Rajan Troll, Alec Radford, Ilya Sutskever, Jan Leike, Jeffrey Wu
related_work.relation: Introduces k-sparse autoencoders and scaling laws. We use their TopK architecture as one of our SAE variants and build on their evaluation methodology.

related_work.title: SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability
related_work.authors: Adam Karvonen, Can Rager, Johnny Lin, Curt Tigges, Joseph Bloom, David Chanin, et al.
related_work.relation: Provides the evaluation framework we use for comparing consensus dictionaries against individual SAE runs. Our consensus metric complements SAEBench by providing a feature-level quality signal.

related_work.title: Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models
related_work.authors: Samuel Marks, Can Rager, Eric J. Michaud, Yonatan Belinkov, David Bau, Aaron Mueller
related_work.relation: Establishes methods for causal evaluation of SAE features via circuit discovery. We adapt their causal evaluation approach to compare consensus vs singleton features.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models
related_work.authors: Hoagy Cunningham, Aidan Ewart, Logan Riggs, Robert Huben, Lee Sharkey
related_work.relation: Foundational paper introducing SAEs for language model interpretability. Establishes that SAE features are more interpretable than neurons.

related_work.title: A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: David Chanin, James Wilken-Smith, Tomas Dulka, Hardik Bhatnagar, Satvik Golechha, Joseph Bloom
related_work.relation: Identifies feature absorption as a failure mode where parent features get absorbed into children. Our consensus approach may help identify absorption-resistant features.

related_work.title: The Geometry of Concepts: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Structure
related_work.authors: Yuxiao Li, Eric J. Michaud, David D. Baek, Joshua Engels, Xiaoqing Sun, Max Tegmark
related_work.relation: Studies geometric structure of SAE features at multiple scales. Our work complements this by connecting geometric properties to stability and causal importance.

related_work.title: Understanding Sparse Autoencoder Scaling in the Presence of Feature Manifolds
related_work.relation: Shows SAEs tile feature manifolds with increasing dictionary size. We hypothesize that singleton features correspond to these manifold tiles, connecting instability to manifold tiling.

related_work.title: Towards Best Practices of Activation Patching in Language Models: Metrics and Methods
related_work.relation: Establishes best practices for causal evaluation via activation patching. We follow their methodology for our feature ablation experiments.

related_work.title: FaithfulSAE: Towards Capturing Faithful Features with Sparse Autoencoders without External Dataset Dependencies
related_work.authors: Seonglae Cho, Harryn Oh, Donghyun Lee, Luis Rodrigues Vieira, Andrew Bermingham, Ziad El Sayed
related_work.relation: Proposes training SAEs on model-generated data to improve faithfulness and seed stability. Complementary to our approach: they improve training, we leverage multi-seed agreement for feature curation.

hypothesis: Sparse autoencoder features that are consistently recovered across multiple independent training runs (consensus features, found in >=80% of runs) are significantly more causally important for model predictions than features found in only one or two runs (singleton features), as measured by KL divergence under feature ablation, sparse probing accuracy, and steering effectiveness. Furthermore, consensus dictionaries (curated to contain only high-consensus features) provide better per-feature utility than full dictionaries from individual SAE runs.
success_criteria: Consensus score significantly predicts causal importance (KL divergence under ablation) with p < 0.01 and effect size Cohen's d > 0.5
Consensus features achieve higher per-feature sparse probing accuracy than singleton features across at least 3 linguistic tasks
Consensus dictionaries match or exceed single-run SAE quality on SAEBench metrics (sparse probing, feature absorption)
Results replicate across both TopK and ReLU+L1 SAE architectures
Singleton features show manifold tiling signatures: higher spatial clustering density and higher pairwise activation correlations compared to consensus features

feasibility.estimated_time: ~7 hours total: 3h SAE training (30 SAEs at ~6min each on Pythia-160M), 1.5h feature matching, 2.5h evaluation
feasibility.model: Pythia-160M (EleutherAI) — small enough for fast SAE training, large enough for meaningful features
feasibility.data: 100M tokens from The Pile for training, 10K sequences for evaluation
